note_data.title: Reversible reactions and equilibrium
keyPoints: A **reversible reaction** is one that can proceed in both the forward and backward directions simultaneously
At **dynamic equilibrium**, the rate of forward reaction equals the rate of backward reaction
The **Haber process** produces ammonia: N₂ + 3H₂ ⇌ 2NH₃ (exothermic reaction)
Haber process conditions: **450°C temperature, 200 atmospheres pressure, iron catalyst**
The **Contact process** produces sulfur trioxide: 2SO₂ + O₂ ⇌ 2SO₃ (exothermic reaction)
Contact process conditions: **450°C temperature, 1-2 atmospheres pressure, vanadium(V) oxide catalyst**
**Le Chatelier's principle**: if conditions change, equilibrium shifts to oppose the change
Increasing pressure favours the side with **fewer gaseous molecules**
**Catalysts** speed up both forward and backward reactions equally, so equilibrium position is unchanged

definitions.definition: A chemical reaction that can proceed in both forward and backward directions
The state where the rate of forward reaction equals the rate of backward reaction, and concentrations remain constant
If a system at equilibrium is subjected to a change, the equilibrium will shift to oppose that change
A substance that increases the rate of a chemical reaction without being permanently changed

howToAnswer.instruction: Give one fact in one sentence. No explanation needed.
Give the point + reason using 'because' or 'this is because'
State what happens + apply Le Chatelier's principle to justify

modelAnswer.question: The Haber process is used to manufacture ammonia. State the balanced equation and explain why high pressure is used in this process.
modelAnswer.answer: N₂ + 3H₂ ⇌ 2NH₃. High pressure is used because there are 4 molecules of reactants but only 2 molecules of product. According to Le Chatelier's principle, high pressure favours the side with fewer molecules, so equilibrium shifts right to produce more ammonia.
markBreakdown: Mark 4: Le Chatelier's principle correctly applied

commonMistakes: Never use → for reversible reactions — always use ⇌ symbol
Students often say 'equilibrium stops' — it's dynamic so reactions continue at equal rates
Don't say catalysts 'shift equilibrium' — they only affect the rate of reaching equilibrium
Never confuse Haber (ammonia) with Contact (sulfur trioxide) process conditions
Students forget that fertiliser production requires ammonia to be converted to ammonium compounds
examinerTips: Reversible reactions appear in every Paper 2 — know both industrial processes thoroughly
Always include the ⇌ symbol when writing reversible reaction equations
Link industrial processes to Pakistan's agricultural needs for fertilisers
Remember that compromise conditions are used in industry to balance yield and rate
note_data.keyDiagramDescription: Energy level diagram showing forward and backward reaction pathways with activation energies, illustrating how catalysts lower activation energy for both directions equally
pastPaperRefs: This topic was tested in M/J 2023 P2 Q4 (6 marks) — they asked students to explain how changing temperature affects the position of equilibrium for the Haber process and predict the effect on ammonia yield
Appeared in O/N 2022 P1 Q15 (1 mark) — multiple choice question asking which factor does NOT affect the position of equilibrium (answer: catalyst)
Featured in M/J 2022 P2 Q3(c) (4 marks) — students had to explain why increasing pressure favours the forward reaction in N₂ + 3H₂ ⇌ 2NH₃
Tested in O/N 2023 P2 Q5 (5 marks) — required explanation of Le Chatelier's principle with reference to the Contact process for sulfuric acid production

workedExample.question: The equilibrium N₂(g) + 3H₂(g) ⇌ 2NH₃(g) ΔH = -92 kJ/mol is established. Explain what happens to the position of equilibrium when: (i) temperature is increased (ii) pressure is increased. [4 marks]
steps: Step 1: Identify that this is an exothermic reaction (ΔH = negative) with unequal moles of gas on each side
Step 2: For temperature increase - apply Le Chatelier's principle: equilibrium shifts to oppose the change
Step 3: For pressure increase - count moles of gas: left side has 4 moles, right side has 2 moles
Step 4: State the shifts and explain using Le Chatelier's principle for both changes
workedExample.answer: (i) When temperature is increased, the equilibrium shifts to the left (backward direction) because the reaction is exothermic and the system opposes the temperature increase by favoring the endothermic reverse reaction. (ii) When pressure is increased, the equilibrium shifts to the right (forward direction) because there are fewer moles of gas on the right side (2 moles) than the left side (4 moles), so the system reduces pressure by favoring the side with fewer gas molecules.
workedExample.markScheme: 1 mark for correct direction of shift for temperature, 1 mark for correct explanation using exothermic nature, 1 mark for correct direction of shift for pressure, 1 mark for explanation using mole ratios

examinerWarnings: Examiner Report 2023: 'Many candidates confused the effect of catalysts on equilibrium position' — always state that catalysts do NOT affect the position of equilibrium, only the rate of reaching equilibrium
Examiner Report 2022: 'Students frequently failed to reference mole ratios when explaining pressure effects' — always count and compare the number of gas molecules on each side of the equation

Examiner Report 2023: 'Weak responses did not link temperature changes to enthalpy values' — always identify whether the reaction is exothermic or endothermic before explaining temperature effects on equilibrium
note_data.keyDiagram: Energy vs Time graph showing: (1) Forward and backward reaction rates starting from different points, (2) Both rates changing over time until they meet at the same level, (3) Horizontal line after intersection point labeled 'Equilibrium reached - rates are equal', (4) Y-axis labeled 'Rate of reaction' and X-axis labeled 'Time', (5) Two curves clearly labeled 'Rate of forward reaction' and 'Rate of backward reaction'. This diagram demonstrates that equilibrium is dynamic with equal rates, not static.
note_data.quickRecap: Equilibrium occurs when forward and backward reaction rates are equal in a closed system. Le Chatelier's principle states that if conditions change, the equilibrium shifts to oppose that change. Increasing temperature favors endothermic direction; increasing pressure favors the side with fewer gas molecules. Catalysts speed up both reactions equally so don't affect equilibrium position, only how quickly it's reached.
